summary: G2 delivers a trait-first storage layer that will, with two structural adjustments, hold up through Phase 2. The KVBackend trait surface, associated Error type, and NodeStore/EdgeStore blanket impls are correctly placed; the indexes + durability + open/create wiring on RedbBackend match R1 triage. Two findings rise to major: (1) ChangeReceiver leaks a concrete pull-shaped receiver into benten-graph's public surface, contradicting the plan's line-605 rule that 'benten-graph has no tokio dep; channel lives in engine'; (2) the ScanResult newtype's Vec-backed shape with public from_vec / IntoIterator<IntoIter=vec::IntoIter> locks the trait into a non-lazy world, which will bite the Phase-2 WASM peer-fetch backend. Three minors flag the DurabilityMode::Group contract-vs-reality gap, the blanket-impl asymmetry for indexes, and the missing explicit 'idempotent put does not duplicate index entries' test. No critical blockers; proceed to G3 after addressing major #1 (move subscribe off RedbBackend inherent) and scheduling major #2 as an R4b ratification.

findings.claim: RedbBackend::subscribe() -> ChangeReceiver couples the storage layer to a pull-shaped channel abstraction, contradicting the plan's explicit rule that benten-graph exposes only the ChangeSubscriber callback trait and the channel concretion lives in benten-engine (plan §ratifications line 605).
findings.detail: The implementation plan resolves the Architect Major #1 refactor as follows: benten-graph ships the ChangeSubscriber trait (push/callback); benten-engine ships a tokio-broadcast impl (native) or a synchronous Vec<Box<dyn ChangeSubscriber>> impl (WASM). This keeps benten-graph runtime-agnostic and keeps the thinness test intact. The current G2 code defines both shapes: ChangeSubscriber trait in store.rs (correct) AND a RedbBackend::subscribe() -> ChangeReceiver pull method + a bare ChangeReceiver struct in lib.rs (incorrect). The pull shape is load-bearing — once it's public, G5 IVM may naturally reach for receiver.recv() on benten-graph alone, which is the exact architecture the R1 refactor forbade. Secondary concern: the ChangeReceiver struct is unit-shaped with a todo!() recv, which means the API is currently unimplementable without smuggling a channel type into benten-graph — the stub's todo-state masks the fact that there is no viable non-channel implementation.
findings.suggested_fix: Delete ChangeReceiver and RedbBackend::subscribe from benten-graph. Replace with a register_subscriber(Arc<dyn ChangeSubscriber>) method on the transaction primitive (G3) so the commit path fans change events out synchronously to registered callbacks. benten-engine then ships a TokioBroadcastSubscriber that wraps a tokio broadcast::Sender and exposes a pull-shaped Receiver to IVM via its own surface. If any test needs a pull-shape receiver inside benten-graph itself, put it behind #[cfg(test)] and leave the production trait push-only. Document this at the top of store.rs so G3 does not accidentally re-introduce the pull coupling when wiring the transaction primitive.

findings.claim: ScanResult's public API (from_vec, into_inner -> Vec, IntoIterator<IntoIter = std::vec::IntoIter<...>>, Deref<Target=[(Vec<u8>,Vec<u8>)]>) concretizes the scan return as an owned Vec in ways that foreclose Phase-2 lazy streaming without a semver break.
findings.detail: The trait-module docstring and the plan both position ScanResult as 'opaque newtype so Phase 2 can swap in a true lazy iterator without breaking callers.' That promise holds only if ScanResult remains shape-opaque. Today it leaks three escape hatches: (a) ScanResult::from_vec is pub — any caller can assert the shape is Vec-backed; (b) into_inner returns Vec, which callers will rely on for zero-copy moves, making any future wrapping type a breaking change; (c) IntoIterator's associated IntoIter type is named as std::vec::IntoIter, so even a tiny change to Box<dyn Iterator> or a custom lazy iterator is an API break on any caller that names the type. The Deref<Target=[(Vec<u8>,Vec<u8>)]> impl is the most damaging — it forces every value to be materialized in a contiguous slab, which a network-fetch WASM backend fundamentally cannot provide without defeating the purpose of the swap. The implementer's R4b-ratification flag is the right instinct; this should not slide to Phase 2.
findings.suggested_fix: Before G3 freezes on the shape: (1) make ScanResult::from_vec pub(crate), keeping FromIterator as the only public constructor; (2) change IntoIter's associated type to an opaque wrapper struct (ScanIter) that internally boxes std::vec::IntoIter today but can hold a Box<dyn Iterator> in Phase 2; (3) remove the Deref<Target=[...]> impl — callers that need slice access can call .as_slice() explicitly and will fail to compile in Phase 2 if we remove it then, which is exactly the signal we want. If that's too aggressive for Phase 1's test surface, at minimum add a #[doc(hidden)] + 'opaque shape, do not rely on slice semantics in Phase 2' warning on the Deref impl, and pin the ratification decision to R4b rather than leaving it floating.

findings.claim: DurabilityMode::Group is a public enum variant that silently collapses to Immediate in the redb mapping. The contract advertises three durability tiers; the implementation offers two. Operators reading the rustdoc ("Group commits into a batched fsync window. Higher throughput, bounded tail latency") will make throughput assumptions that do not hold.
findings.detail: This is a classic contract-vs-reality gap that survives until an operator benchmarks with Group mode and discovers zero difference from Immediate. The docstring on the enum variant promises throughput behavior the implementation does not deliver. Three remediation options: (a) drop Group from the enum until redb grows batched-fsync — cleanest; (b) redefine Group to mean 'application-layer batched' with an explicit buffer/flush API on RedbBackend — more work but genuinely different from Immediate; (c) leave it and update the docstring to say 'Phase 1: equivalent to Immediate; reserved for a future redb version.' Even option (c) needs the warning visible on the variant's rustdoc, not tucked inside to_redb_durability.
findings.suggested_fix: Pick option (a) for Phase 1: remove the Group variant. Downstream tests that exercised Group mode can either be deleted or retargeted to Immediate + Async. If removal is deemed premature, at minimum amend the variant's rustdoc with '**Phase 1: collapses to Immediate.** redb v4 does not yet expose batched-fsync; this variant is reserved for a future mapping.' and add a debug_assert or log::warn when Group is selected so operators see the gap at runtime. Re-evaluate in Phase 2 when redb may have grown the primitive.

findings.location: layering — crates/benten-graph/src/redb_backend.rs inherent put_node/delete_node vs. crates/benten-graph/src/store.rs blanket NodeStore
findings.claim: Index maintenance lives on RedbBackend inherent methods that shadow the NodeStore blanket impl. Any future KVBackend (in-memory mock, WASM peer-fetch) that uses the blanket impl will silently lose index maintenance, and the asymmetry is invisible at the call site.
findings.detail: The blanket NodeStore::put_node writes only n:CID -> bytes. The inherent RedbBackend::put_node writes n:CID + every label-index entry + every property-index entry atomically. Rust method resolution picks the inherent when both are in scope, so RedbBackend users are fine. But: (1) a test or bench that calls <Self as NodeStore>::put_node on a RedbBackend (as the inherent get_node already does at redb_backend.rs:368) bypasses the index; (2) any future backend implementing KVBackend will only get the blanket impl — so the MemBackend in the trait-module doctest ends up with no indexes, which is probably not the invariant we want to advertise. The deeper architectural question: should indexes be a KVBackend-agnostic concern that lives alongside NodeStore/EdgeStore (writing index keys via the trait's own put), or are they redb-specific (MultimapTable is a redb type)? Today's choice — redb-specific — means multi-backend parity is lost.
findings.suggested_fix: Two options. (a) Promote label + property indexes to the blanket impl by encoding them as plain KV pairs (li:label|CID -> empty, pi:label|prop|value_bytes|CID -> empty) that any KVBackend gets for free. This drops the MultimapTable dependency but requires a scan+filter where today MultimapTable::get is O(log N + matches). (b) Keep MultimapTable (redb-optimized) but either extract a LabelIndexPolicy trait so other backends can plug in their own index storage, or document explicitly that the blanket NodeStore impl is 'unindexed — use RedbBackend for indexed workloads.' Pick (a) if Phase 2 WASM / in-memory mock need index parity; (b) if Phase 1 is the only place other backends are viable and indexing is a redb feature. Either way, add a test that calls `<RedbBackend as NodeStore>::put_node` and asserts the index was NOT updated, to lock the current asymmetry into the spec rather than leaving it accidental.

findings.location: crates/benten-graph/src/redb_backend.rs:320-348 + tests — missing 'idempotent put does not duplicate index entries' coverage
findings.claim: Content-addressed put is idempotent at the node-body level (n:CID overwrites itself), but the test suite does not lock the parallel invariant for the indexes: a second put of the same Node must leave exactly one label-index entry and one property-index entry, not two.
findings.detail: MultimapTable::insert(k, v) is set-semantics — re-inserting the same (key, value) pair is a no-op — so the current code is probably correct. But that's a property of redb's MultimapTable, not something the tests prove. Two concerns: (1) if a future refactor swaps MultimapTable for a BTreeSet-on-bytes or a range-encoded layout, duplicate-insert behavior may silently regress; (2) the 'two Nodes with Float(0.0) and Float(-0.0) produce same CID' invariant from benten-core flows into 'same index entry' here, and that chain isn't tested end-to-end. The second property is especially worth locking because it's the exact kind of cross-crate invariant that bitrots when G1 and G2 evolve independently.
findings.suggested_fix: Add two tests to crates/benten-graph/src/redb_backend.rs tests or a new idempotence.rs integration test: (a) `put_node_twice_same_cid_leaves_single_index_entry` — insert the canonical test node twice, assert get_by_label returns a single CID (not two) and get_by_property returns a single CID; (b) `neg_zero_float_indexes_identically_to_positive_zero` — build two Nodes whose only difference is Value::Float(0.0) vs Value::Float(-0.0), put both, assert both resolve to the same CID, and assert get_by_property with either value returns exactly one CID. These lock invariants that currently live only in docstrings.

findings.location: crates/benten-graph/src/redb_backend.rs — RedbBackend vs. WriteContext + Transaction + ChangeReceiver + SnapshotHandle stubs
findings.claim: RedbBackend's public API advertises four stubs (put_node_with_context, transaction, transaction_with_deny_on_commit, subscribe, snapshot) that all todo!() today. This is fine for the TDD red-phase, but the choice of putting those methods inherent on RedbBackend (rather than on a future EngineInternal trait or on a G3-owned Transactor) means G3 will have to land code onto this type directly — coupling benten-graph's only concrete backend to the transaction primitive.
findings.detail: This is a structural debt flag, not a correctness issue. Today: Transaction, WriteContext, ChangeReceiver, SnapshotHandle all live in benten-graph::lib and are consumed by RedbBackend methods. When G3 lands, it will either (a) implement the transaction primitive inside benten-graph by filling in these stubs — which keeps transaction + redb atomic commit tightly coupled — or (b) move the transaction primitive into benten-engine and leave benten-graph with just the KVBackend + change-emission hooks. Option (b) is architecturally cleaner (G3's begin/commit/rollback closure shape, abort-reason capture, and Drop-for-rollback semantics compose better with the capability pre-write hook that lives in benten-caps), but the current stub placement signals option (a). Flag so G3 dispatches consciously choose one and document it, rather than defaulting to 'keep filling in the stubs where they are.'
findings.suggested_fix: Before G3 dispatches, add a short ADR-style note in crates/benten-graph/README.md or the plan's §G3 section documenting which of (a) or (b) G3 chooses, with one sentence of rationale. If (b), delete the todo-shaped inherent methods on RedbBackend in G3's first commit and move Transaction / WriteContext / ChangeReceiver / SnapshotHandle into benten-engine (or a new benten-graph::transaction submodule that has no redb dependency). Either choice is defensible; leaving it implicit is the risk.

findings.claim: Three interfaces in G2 are the most likely to break in Phase 2; flagging them so G3-G8 consumers code defensively.
findings.detail: (1) ScanResult concrete shape — see finding g2-ar-2; consumers should treat it as IntoIterator-only and never name the IntoIter type. (2) ChangeEvent::label as String (single primary label) rather than labels: Vec<String> — the store.rs docstring already admits reconsideration in Phase 2 if the IVM view surface demands multi-label events; any consumer should destructure via pattern rather than positional field access so the Vec migration is a one-line change. (3) KVBackend::put_batch taking &[(Vec<u8>, Vec<u8>)] rather than &[BatchOp] — the current shape is Put-only; when G3's transaction primitive composes heterogeneous writes (node put + edge delete + index remove), the signature has to grow, and the BatchOp enum is already in scope precisely to absorb that change. Consumers should route all batch writes through the transaction primitive rather than calling put_batch directly so the eventual signature shift is invisible.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a docstring at the top of crates/benten-graph/src/lib.rs listing these three 'Phase 2 evolution points' so downstream group leads see them without having to re-derive the list from critic reports. Example: 'Phase 2 evolution: ScanResult is shape-opaque (do not name IntoIter); ChangeEvent.label may become labels (destructure by name); put_batch may migrate to BatchOp (prefer the transaction primitive for new call sites).' This costs nothing and saves later-group implementers from accidentally baking in churn.

findings.claim: The Self::Error: std::error::Error + Send + Sync + 'static bound is permissive enough for a future network-fetch backend today, but the trait does not express the async-ness a peer-fetch backend needs. This is a Phase 2 latent issue worth explicit acknowledgment.
findings.detail: A WASM/iroh peer-fetch backend would return Future<Output = Result<...>> rather than Result<...> directly. The current trait is synchronous — get, put, delete, scan, put_batch all return Result directly. For Phase 1 that's exactly right (redb is sync; the integration tests are sync; cargo-nextest is sync). For Phase 2, the trait will either (a) grow async mirror methods (async fn get_async ...) with a default impl that blocks on the sync one, or (b) split into sync-KVBackend + async-KVBackend traits with a bridge, or (c) go all-in on async-trait / native async-fn-in-trait and port redb's sync API behind block_in_place. The bound permissiveness on Error is fine; the async concern is separate and not addressable in G2 without overfitting. Flag so Phase 2 planning does not assume 'KVBackend works as-is for network backends.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single-sentence note to the KVBackend docstring: 'Phase 1 KVBackend is synchronous. Phase 2 network-backed implementations (WASM peer-fetch, iroh-fetch) will require either async method variants or a parallel async-KVBackend trait; the Error bound is already permissive enough for the error types those backends need.' No code change in G2.

notes: Scope check: I did not line-level-audit the NodeStore/EdgeStore edge index round-trip (code-reviewer's lens) or the specific redb v4 API usage (benten-core-guardian's lens — though the MultimapTable choice is correct). Confirmed in passing: ErrorCode catalog has BackendNotFound, TxAborted, NestedTransactionNotSupported, SystemZoneWrite (all present in error_code.rs); GraphError::code() maps every variant (Redb and Decode fall through to ErrorCode::Unknown('graph_internal') which is acceptable for a stringified backend error); WriteContext::is_privileged field exists and is consumed by both crates/benten-graph/tests/system_zone.rs and crates/benten-engine/tests/system_zone_api_exclusivity.rs (SC1 plumbing is in place for G3 to wire). The two major findings are the actionable ones — the rest are documentation/test locks that can ride alongside G3. No critical blockers; verdict 'revise' hinges on addressing g2-ar-1 (subscribe off RedbBackend) before G3 freezes the transaction primitive's change-emission shape, and g2-ar-2 (ScanResult shape tightening) before any external caller materializes the Vec assumption.
